nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving freshwater streams, ponds, lakes, rivers, and canals — typically nutrient-rich, shallow waters of the Amazon basin

decorPreferences: room for emersed growth above the waterline (optional)

summary: A bright-green, feathery-leaved stem plant from the Amazon basin with whorled, fir-tree-like foliage that often emerges above the waterline; cold-tolerant and pond-suitable, but invasive and legally restricted in many regions.
careNotes: Myriophyllum aquaticum, commonly called Parrot's Feather, is a perennial stem plant native to the Amazon River basin of South America. It produces whorled (4–6 per node), finely divided, feathery leaves; submerged foliage is typically bright chartreuse-green, while emersed stems and leaves are darker blue-green and more pronouncedly feathery, often rising 15–30 cm above the water surface. The plant grows readily both submerged and emersed. Sources generally place healthy submerged height at roughly 15–40 cm, with emergent stems capable of much greater length in open ponds. Tropica does not list M. aquaticum specifically (it stocks M. mattogrossense and M. sp. 'Guyana'), so cross-verification used Buce Plant, aqua-fish.net, the RHS plant database, and Wikipedia. Sources broadly agree on water parameters of around 18–28 °C, pH 6.0–7.5 (tolerant up to ~8.0), and moderate-to-hard water (gH ~5–16). Buce Plant rates lighting as moderate-to-high and aqua-fish.net calls for bright lighting; both consider CO2 supplementation optional but beneficial. Growth rate is reported as fast under good conditions. The plant is unusually cold-tolerant for a tropical aquarium species — RHS rates it as hardy through most of the UK (H4) — and can be overwintered outdoors in many temperate ponds. INVASIVE STATUS: This species is widely regarded as invasive and is legally restricted in many jurisdictions. In the United Kingdom it is listed on Schedule 9 of the Wildlife & Countryside Act and is banned from sale; the EU has listed it as an invasive alien species of Union concern since 2016. In the United States, Buce Plant notes it cannot be shipped to IL, IN, MI, MN, NY, OH, PA, WA, or WI, and additional sources report sale bans in AL, CT, MA, ME, and VT. Australia and New Zealand also list it as a noxious aquatic weed. Hobbyists must check local regulations before acquiring this plant, and disposal of trimmings should be by drying/composting, never into waterways. Outside South America, populations are reproductively female-only and propagate exclusively via stem and rhizome fragments — making escape into the wild trivially easy. Stems are somewhat brittle; handle gently when planting and trimming. The plant is also sensitive to substrate-digging fish.
breedingNotes: Propagated by stem cuttings: take 5–15 cm terminal cuttings, remove the lowest whorl of leaves, and replant gently into nutrient-rich substrate. Lateral side-shoots and rhizome fragments also root readily. Outside its native South American range, all known plants are female and reproduction is entirely vegetative — which is precisely why discarded fragments establish so easily in the wild and underpin the species' invasive status. Dispose of trimmings by drying or composting, never by releasing into waterways.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Manifest-supplied Tropica search URL did not surface a Tropica species page for M. aquaticum (Tropica catalogues M. mattogrossense and M. sp. 'Guyana' but not M. aquaticum), so Buce Plant — the manifest's designated secondary — was promoted to primary. Buce Plant lists the species at moderate-to-high light, CO2 recommended for best growth, pH 6.0–7.5, mature height 4–16 in (10–40 cm), and provides the invasive-species shipping-restriction notice (cannot ship to IL, IN, MI, MN, NY, OH, PA, WA, WI).

additional.name: aqua-fish.net
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (23–28 °C optimum, tolerant 18–28 °C), pH (6.0–8.0), water hardness (8–16 dGH), bright lighting requirement, optional CO2, fast growth, submerged height 15–30 cm, and family Haloragaceae. Rates difficulty as easy-to-moderately-difficult; combined with the invasive-species concern this entry uses careLevel = intermediate.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Haloragaceae, order Saxifragales), native range (South America / Amazon basin), morphology (whorls of 4–6 feathery leaves; emersed leaves resemble small fir trees), and invasive status (banned in UK/EU since 2016; US bans in AL, CT, MA, ME, VT, WA). Confirms reproduction outside South America is asexual via fragments and all plants are female.

additional.name: Royal Horticultural Society
additional.notes: Cross-checked invasive listing (Schedule 9 of the UK Wildlife & Countryside Act, banned from sale) and cold-tolerance (hardiness rating H4, hardy through most of the UK without winter protection) — supporting the pond-suitable, cold-tolerant note in careNotes.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Listed as an invasive aquatic weed in many jurisdictions: banned from sale in the UK (Schedule 9 of the Wildlife & Countryside Act) and EU (invasive alien species of Union concern since 2016); sale or shipping restricted in multiple US states (including IL, IN, MI, MN, NY, OH, PA, WA, WI, AL, CT, MA, ME, VT) and listed as a noxious weed in Australia and New Zealand. Hobbyists must verify local regulations before acquiring this plant and never dispose of trimmings into waterways.
